response:
D

The question asks which substance retains the largest amount of energy from the Sun, meaning which has the highest capacity to absorb and hold onto solar (thermal) energy rather than reflecting it away.

- Snow (A) has very high albedo (reflectivity), bouncing back most sunlight as visible and infrared radiation. It absorbs very little energy.
- Sand (B) absorbs more than snow but still reflects a significant portion (especially light-colored sand) and has relatively low specific heat capacity, so it heats up quickly but does not "retain" (store) large amounts of energy per degree of temperature rise.
- Grass (C) absorbs well in visible wavelengths (appears green because it reflects that part), but like sand it has moderate specific heat and loses heat through evapotranspiration and convection. It does not store energy as effectively as water.
- Water (D) has the highest specific heat capacity of the four (~4.18 J/g°C). It absorbs a large fraction of incoming solar radiation (especially when dark or deep) and can store enormous amounts of thermal energy with only modest temperature change. This is why large bodies of water moderate climate so effectively.

Thus water retains/stores by far the largest amount of solar energy.
